userMessage: A1 A2 A3 A4 what do they tell us

response.text:
Looking at the screenshot carefully, here is exactly what A1, A2, A3, and A4 tell us:

---

## What Are A1–A4?

These are **measurement annotation markers** — the scope automatically places them on the waveform to show **where each measurement was taken**. They correspond to **Meas 4 (Eye Width)** specifically, as the label says **"A1 & (A2 | A3) & A4"** — meaning the eye width measurement requires all four anchor points to be valid simultaneously.

---

## A1 — Far Left, Red X ❌

- Located at approximately **-100 ns**, before the trigger point
- The **red X** means this anchor point **failed** — the scope could not find a valid measurement reference here
- This is the **left edge / start boundary** of the eye width measurement window
- Why it failed: the signal at that point is in the middle of a transition or the scope didn't have enough bit history to lock a valid crossing

---

## A2 — Green Check ✅ (~50–100 ns)

- Located just after the trigger point, on the **first rising edge**
- The **green checkmark** means this anchor **succeeded**
- This marks the **left crossing point** of the eye — where the signal crosses the mid-level (1.658 V) going up
- The **90%/10% markers** are also visible right here — this is where rise time was measured too
- This is a clean, well-defined edge the scope could lock onto

---

## A3 — Green Check ✅ (~250–280 ns)

- Located on what appears to be a **falling edge** around 250–280 ns
- The **green checkmark** means this anchor **succeeded**
- This marks the **right crossing point** of the eye — where the signal crosses the mid-level going down
- Together with A2, the scope measured: **Eye Width = A3 time − A2 time = 24.835 ns**
- That 24.835 ns is the horizontal opening of the eye at the mid-level crossing

---

## A4 — Far Right, Red X ❌

- Located at approximately **600–700 ns**, near the right edge of the capture
- The **red X** means this anchor point **failed**
- This is the **right edge / end boundary** of the eye width measurement window
- Why it failed: the signal is in a long stable HIGH state at that point (~3.2 V) — there is no crossing event for the scope to anchor to
- The scope needs a transition (crossing) here and didn't find one

---

## What A1–A4 Tell Us Together

| Marker | Position | Status | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| A1 | ~-100 ns | ❌ Failed | No valid left boundary crossing found |
| A2 | ~75 ns | ✅ Passed | Left eye crossing — rising edge at mid-level |
| A3 | ~265 ns | ✅ Passed | Right eye crossing — falling edge at mid-level |
| A4 | ~650 ns | ❌ Failed | No valid right boundary crossing found |

### Key Inferences:
1. **Only 1 eye width measurement was made** (Pop = 1) because A1 and A4 both failed — the scope only found **one valid pair** (A2 + A3) in the entire capture
2. **The eye width of 24.835 ns has zero statistical confidence** — it is a single measurement, no mean/std dev possible
3. **The signal is sparse** — there aren't enough transitions in this single-shot capture to build rich eye statistics
4. **The long flat HIGH region** (~400–700 ns) is why A4 failed — no crossing = no anchor
5. **This is why Single acquisition mode is limiting** — a continuous or averaged acquisition would give far more A markers, more eye width samples, and a statistically valid result

---

## Bottom Line on A1–A4:
The scope found **only one valid eye width event** in the entire capture. The two outer markers failed because the signal edges weren't where the algorithm expected them. You need more transitions — switch from **Single to Normal or Average acquisition** to populate the eye with more data and get meaningful Eye Width statistics.
